item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 just for how long it probably took to do this without using Anglo Saxon symboll #5

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wrong on a couple of points here: Anglo-Saxon(aka Old English) used a modified Latin alphabet that retained a couple of runes from the Futhorc. English itself is a Frisian language that has picked up a lot of bootleg words and grammatical cruft from Germanic and Romantic languages over the centuries.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With a quick google, you can tell Anglo-Saxon Symbol #5 is equivalent to "R". "E" is #19. Also, English is Latin/Greek-based. It doesn't use the same symbols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. John Griffin: … l'oblation d'une batavia

:(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the letter "E" is even more prevalent in French than in English, this article was deemed untranslatable.

Challenge accepted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a cool skip, you did a good job of writing it in its format, and typing this discussion thing without using anglo saxon symbol #5 is hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It took me a while to realise that none of the words in this SCP had the letter 'e' in it.

Good job mate.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty much just upvoting as congratulations for the lipogram. Having been reminded of the other articles upthread, this one doesn't really add anything beyond what they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing much of the above: this is Gasby as an SCP (and orders of magnitude less impressive thereby); the Futhark reference is incorrect; they could simply use a substitute character; this weird philosophical discipline that causes weird stuff to happen if you use the letter "e" is too poorly developed to suspend disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels like a less funny and more obvious version of the eyephone, and not as impressive as Gadsby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know how I feel about this. Seems too much like the screw is more important than the wood (I'm thinking buffalos, safes and any other scip where the gimmick outweighs the narrative).

Specific example: some researcher writes up a technical document entirely using words that don't contain the letter E, because bad things will happen.

If I were that researcher's boss, I'd say: Why didn't you just write a normal document, and just substitute the e's with an @ or & or some other symbol, whilst making it clear what that symbol represents?

You could argue that doing so increases the risk of accidentally typing an E, but it just seems very contrived.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The phrase "Anglo-Saxon Symbol #5" confused me, because I thought it would mean ᚱ (rād, i.e. "Anglo-Saxon R"). The symbols A, B, C, etc. aren't Anglo-Saxon, or even English, they are Latin.

Anyway, because it confused me, I didn't get it at all. I would suggest another phrase, like "Latin script #5" or "ISO-8859-1 symbols 69 and 101" (E and e).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
was advocating curing constipation by sacrificing salad to Odin on a pagan altar

This is where I lost it. This is so random compared to the other two entries, which made some sense.

And you pretty much just spelled out at the end what was so obvious from the beginning with this certain letter rhyming with "B".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the effort taken to create a work with such a bizarre drawback, and I quite enjoyed the variations you've come up with here. However, the explanatory note at the end leaves me questioning the point of the format screw in the first place. Yeah, not every anomalous property needs a reason to exist, per se, but when it comes to format scews, I find this is a must. Right now, I'm not seeing a reason for this to exist; the introduction just seems generically vague/creepy without really justifying the absence of 'e'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This rides the gimmick (which as noted upthread has been done previously in Series III) way too hard. Additionally, the note at the end strikes me as lazy. There's references to a "hiding world" and "bad things occurring," which are dropped so vaguely and with so little setup that I suspect you don't know what they are, and in any event they're not anything to the reader other than nebulous spookiness put there for indeterminate reasons.

Also, it's hard to be impressed with an article pulling this trick off when there's an entire novel that does this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, but that one was funny. This is really disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I am super impressed with the level of effort that this had to have been in order to write this all out without the 'E'. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The absence herein contained regarding E exceptionally elevates end-user cognitive expression, specifically regarding the nature contained object represents. Explaining the anomalies regarding items reducing language efficacy remains extraordinarily mentally intensive. Excellent essay, friend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I was pretty sure it was the most commonly used! I just didn't want to say it, in case I was wrong. Kudos to author for succeeding!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty badass. Nice job, Observer.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the format screw, there isn't really a lot going on here to make it interesting in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-2010

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not one the most commonly-used letters in English. It is the most commonly-used letter in the English language. So writing this without using an "e" even once is really hard to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh this is so cool. I love it so much. E is one of the most common letters in english (I think), so I can hardly imagine trying to write an article without it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. At first I was truly hazy about the notion of this artifact, but looking into your discussion saying, I grasp that this topic would turn out indubitably difficult. Quality work sir.

+1

(I can't imagine writing a whole damn article like that ._.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My main inspiration for this one was the brief paragraph of e-less containment procedures in SCP-2334.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, but that one was a lot sillier. This one is honestly kinda creepy.

Any chance this is related to the Order of the Four Vowels?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't we have one like this, but for "i"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creative, love how that no E/e can be used in words in presence or writing of this SCP. Still it confused me, but hella good.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies for the short length, but this was a bitch to write without using e. Hopefully it's still interesting as is.
